narrative.headline: Florida is expanding both jobs and labor supply at the same time.
whatStandsOut: Nonfarm Payrolls are up 15.8k over the last year.
Labor Force is up 37,536 people year-over-year.
improving: Unemployment remains low by historical standards despite a 1.1 point annual increase.
Private Education & Health Services is among the strongest job growers (2.3% YoY).
Professional & Business Services is among the strongest job growers (0.8% YoY).
Leisure & Hospitality is among the strongest job growers (0.1% YoY).
whyItMatters: A growing labor force plus payroll expansion signals depth, not just short-term demand spikes.
Sector breadth helps Florida absorb national volatility while keeping opportunity distributed.
Population gains reinforce long-run demand for housing, services, and infrastructure investment.

narrative.headline: Florida’s innovation stack is expanding alongside its development engine.
signals: Professional & business services employment is up 0.8% year-over-year.
development: Real gross state product is up 11.9% over three years.
momentum: Innovation metrics are intended as directional signal layers, not standalone verdicts.
Business formation plus advanced-service employment growth is a strong expansion pattern.
Development capacity and innovation capacity should be tracked together for policy and capital allocation.

brainNotes.title: Is Florida missing the AI capex boom?
brainNotes.summary: Florida's 4.8% unemployment rate sits beside a +40,500 payroll month. The next question is whether Texas and other states are capturing the infrastructure-heavy AI layer Florida has not yet measured.

sources.label: CBRE North America Data Center Trends

brainNotes.title: Strategic compute, not dumb load.
brainNotes.summary: SB 484 makes the ratepayer case explicit: data centers should cover their own power, transmission, and water costs. The harder product question is how Florida competes for strategic compute without shifting the bill to households.

brainNotes.title: The Florida lane is edge, LATAM, Space Coast.
brainNotes.summary: Florida does not need to copy a Texas training-campus strategy. The stronger lane is inference near population, LATAM gateways, Space Coast aerospace compute, and resilient edge capacity that pays its own way.

strategy.headline: Florida needs a strategy cockpit, not another data portal.
strategy.summary: This layer benchmarks Florida against competitor states, translates outside dashboard models into product moves, and frames the next-economy question around peer states, clusters, talent, metros, and scenarios.

peerStates.name: Florida
peerStates.positioning: The scale state with migration, trade, aerospace, and founder formation.
peerStates.watch: Whether population and income migration convert into high-wage payroll depth.

peerStates.name: Texas
peerStates.positioning: The capex and power-heavy benchmark for energy, chips, data centers, and advanced manufacturing.
peerStates.watch: Whether Texas keeps turning infrastructure scale into payroll and wage advantage.

peerStates.name: Georgia
peerStates.positioning: The logistics, film, auto, battery, and Atlanta talent benchmark.
peerStates.watch: Whether Georgia keeps converting industrial incentives into high-quality job growth.

peerStates.name: North Carolina
peerStates.positioning: The county-vitality and research-triangle benchmark for balanced growth.
peerStates.watch: Whether research, manufacturing, and county momentum stay synchronized.

peerStates.name: Tennessee
peerStates.positioning: The education-to-employment and advanced manufacturing benchmark.
peerStates.watch: Whether its talent pipeline keeps pace with industrial project growth.

peerStates.name: Arizona
peerStates.positioning: The semiconductor, data-center, and desert-growth peer.
peerStates.watch: Whether power, water, and chip capex translate into durable wage gains.

peerStates.name: Utah
peerStates.positioning: The high-growth, high-participation tech and family-formation peer.
peerStates.watch: Whether a smaller state can keep outperforming larger talent markets.

peerStates.name: California
peerStates.positioning: The incumbent innovation economy Florida keeps measuring itself against.
peerStates.watch: Whether capital and talent leakage continues, or California re-accelerates.

benchmarkExamples.name: Texas 2036
benchmarkExamples.takeaway: Make the dashboard answer where the state is headed, not just what happened last month.

benchmarkExamples.name: Pennsylvania On Target
benchmarkExamples.takeaway: Organize growth around sectors, supply chains, workforce gaps, and emerging industries.

benchmarkExamples.name: North Carolina EVI
benchmarkExamples.takeaway: Rank local momentum so statewide leaders can see where growth is broadening or narrowing.

benchmarkExamples.name: Tennessee E2E
benchmarkExamples.takeaway: Tie degrees and credentials to jobs and wage outcomes, not just enrollment.

benchmarkExamples.name: Massachusetts Competitiveness Index
benchmarkExamples.takeaway: Put Florida in the ring with competitor states across business, labor, migration, and quality-of-life metrics.

clusters.title: AI capex and power readiness
clusters.thesis: Florida should track whether it is missing the power-heavy AI infrastructure boom while peers absorb larger data-center and semiconductor projects.
clusters.bottleneck: Power, water, transmission, and whether incentives distinguish strategic compute from dumb load.
clusters.proof: The active Florida Brain thesis is that unemployment softness may partly reflect missed industrial capex, not just a normal labor cycle.
clusters.whatToTrack: Data-center megawatts, interconnection queue, industrial power rates, project capex, and high-wage construction plus operations jobs.

clusters.title: Space Coast aerospace cadence
clusters.thesis: Florida's most distinctive innovation cluster is physical: launches, factories, spaceport infrastructure, and dual-use manufacturing.
clusters.bottleneck: Specialized talent, industrial sites, supplier depth, and how much of the value chain stays in Florida.
clusters.proof: The dashboard already tracks 109 launches, a $6B aerospace pipeline, and Blue Origin's $600M Cape Canaveral expansion.
clusters.whatToTrack: Launch cadence, aerospace payrolls, project pipeline, supplier announcements, and advanced manufacturing wage growth.

clusters.title: LATAM gateway and logistics
clusters.thesis: South Florida's gateway claim is strongest where trade, finance, aviation, containers, and relationships stack together.
clusters.bottleneck: Cold-chain capacity, port throughput, customs efficiency, insurance, and last-mile infrastructure.
clusters.proof: PortMiami and Port Everglades give the dashboard a physical trade base instead of a slogan.
clusters.whatToTrack: TEUs, tonnage, refrigerated cargo, LATAM share, air cargo, export categories, and bilateral trade via Florida ports and airports.

clusters.title: Talent pipeline and wage outcomes
clusters.thesis: Florida cannot become the next-economy state if credentials, degrees, and STEM labor supply do not map to target clusters.
clusters.bottleneck: The dashboard still needs education-to-employment outcomes by region and industry.
clusters.proof: Tennessee and Washington show the better model: talent supply and wage outcomes belong next to economic ambition.
clusters.whatToTrack: Degrees, credentials, target occupations, wage outcomes, STEM gaps, and regional placement into priority clusters.

talentPipeline.title: The next dashboard gap is education-to-employment proof.
talentPipeline.summary: The benchmark scan says Floridanomics should connect credentials, degrees, target occupations, and wage outcomes. That turns workforce from a talking point into a strategy surface.

stats.label: Model to steal
stats.context: Education programs linked to employment and wages one to five years after graduation

stats.label: Supply-demand model
stats.context: STEM talent supply measured against workforce demand

stats.label: Florida target
stats.context: Degrees, credentials, jobs, and wages mapped to aerospace, AI, logistics, life sciences, and fintech

interpretation: The talent question is not whether Florida has people. The question is whether the state's education and credential pipeline maps to the clusters it says it wants to win.

This should become a source-linked module with program output, regional job demand, wage outcomes, and cluster placement. That is the missing bridge between Florida Brain narrative and workforce policy.

sources.label: Washington STEM Talent Supply and Demand Dashboard

scenarios.label: Base case
scenarios.summary: Florida keeps gaining people, income, and company formation, but high-wage cluster depth grows unevenly.
signals: Labor force keeps expanding while unemployment stays elevated from recent lows.
Business formation remains strong but information employment is not yet escape velocity.
Trade and aerospace carry distinctive strength, but AI infrastructure remains under-measured.

scenarios.label: Ambition case
scenarios.summary: Florida captures more AI, aerospace, LATAM logistics, life-sciences, and advanced-services investment without subsidizing dumb load.
signals: Data-center and grid investments show up as high-wage construction and operations jobs.
Space Coast suppliers deepen the aerospace value chain inside Florida.
Talent pipeline data begins showing cluster-specific wage and placement gains.

scenarios.label: Risk case
scenarios.summary: Florida keeps the migration story but misses too much of the power-heavy capex and STEM employment boom.
signals: Unemployment rises while payroll growth concentrates in lower-wage or population-serving sectors.
Texas, Georgia, Arizona, and North Carolina absorb more industrial and data-center investment.
Florida Brain has to explain why a high-growth state is not leading the high-wage cycle.

competition.headline: Florida needs a competitor-state machine, not a brag book.
competition.summary: The SelectFlorida archive shows the next product frontier: compare Florida against the states winning FDI, mega-project tools, institutional capacity, migration, and semiconductor commitments. This layer turns those files into an operating surface for Florida Brain.

vaultLog.caveat: Internal SelectFlorida and EFI archive discovery. Refresh each public stat against current primary sources before external publication.

metroComparison.headline: South Florida is the scale bet. Austin is the velocity warning. Seattle and Boston are the productivity benchmarks.
metroComparison.summary: This domestic layer compares operating regions, not state slogans. Florida needs to see its own metros against the few U.S. markets that actually teach us something: software velocity, research density, mega-market scale, healthcare migration, and elite productivity.
metroComparison.asOf: BLS LAUS March 2026, BLS CES April 2026, QCEW 2025 Q3 and 2024 annual industry data

regions.name: South Florida MSA
regions.verdict: Scale up, payroll soft, AI base still catching up.
regions.read: South Florida has the people, firms, capital, and LATAM gateway position. The gap is converting that scale into a deeper AI-convertible technical and R&D employment base.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: +9.1% over five years, the largest Florida labor pool and bigger than Austin, Seattle, Boston, or Nashville.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: April 2026 payrolls slipped slightly even though the five-year payroll gain remains +14.2%.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: Meaningful finance, professional-services, information, and HQ base, but less concentrated than Austin, Seattle, or Boston.

signals.label: Software depth
signals.detail: The software share is the clearest gap versus Austin's 5.2% and Seattle's 3.1%.

regions.name: Tampa Bay MSA
regions.verdict: Good AI-exposed share, but labor and covered employment are soft.
regions.read: Tampa is the Florida metro that most deserves to be in the domestic comparison set after Miami. It has real business-services and finance depth, but the recent labor signal is not yet an Austin-style breakout.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: +8.7% over five years, roughly Austin-scale but with softer recent labor-force momentum.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: Solid five-year payroll growth, but only +0.1% year over year in April 2026.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: A surprisingly strong Florida internal competitor on finance, professional services, information, and HQ exposure.

signals.label: Software depth
signals.detail: Better than South Florida on share, but still well below Austin.

regions.name: Orlando MSA
regions.read: Orlando is Florida's domestic velocity story: faster labor-force growth than South Florida, better five-year payroll growth, and a plausible applied-AI lane around simulation, defense, training, hospitality tech, and space adjacency.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: +15.2% over five years, the strongest Florida labor-force velocity in this set.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: The Florida metro closest to Austin-style payroll momentum.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: Less concentrated than South Florida or Tampa, but improving from a different base.

signals.label: R&D signal
signals.detail: Not Boston, but stronger than South Florida on the QCEW R&D share.

regions.name: Jacksonville MSA
regions.verdict: Strategic platform, but not yet a velocity market.
regions.read: Jacksonville matters for logistics, port strategy, finance, and defense adjacency. On current labor and payroll momentum, though, it is not yet the domestic benchmark for the AI-productivity story.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: +7.1% over five years, useful but much smaller than South Florida, Tampa, or Orlando.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: April 2026 payrolls and 2025 Q3 covered employment were both down year over year.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: The AI-convertible private employment share is materially below the other Florida metros.

signals.label: Wage base
signals.detail: Covered wages are close to Tampa and above Orlando, but still below South Florida and the national tech peers.

regions.name: Austin MSA
regions.verdict: Smaller than South Florida, but moving faster and more technical.
regions.read: Austin is the warning light. It is not bigger than South Florida, but it has faster five-year labor and payroll growth plus a much denser software employment base.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: Roughly half South Florida's labor-force scale, but +21.5% over five years.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: Five-year payroll growth is the cleanest domestic velocity benchmark in this set.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: Austin has a more concentrated AI-convertible private employment base.

signals.label: Software depth
signals.detail: Austin's software share is more than four times South Florida's share.

regions.name: Seattle MSA
regions.verdict: Labor momentum is soft, but the productivity base is elite.
regions.read: Seattle shows the difference between labor velocity and productivity power. Its labor market is cooling, but the wage and AI-exposed employment base remains the benchmark for a mature productivity machine.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: Large enough to matter, but smaller than South Florida and down 0.9% year over year.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: Payroll momentum is nearly flat and QCEW covered employment was down 0.8% year over year in 2025 Q3.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: The highest AI-exposed private employment share in this domestic set.

signals.label: Wage power
signals.detail: Weighted annual pay across the AI-exposed stack is dramatically above South Florida and Austin.

regions.name: Boston MSA
regions.verdict: Weak labor velocity, elite research density.
regions.read: Boston is not the growth-speed benchmark. It is the research-density benchmark: universities, biotech, R&D services, and high-wage technical employment that Florida has to learn from without copying wholesale.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: Big, but labor force was down 2.2% year over year in March 2026.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: Boston is not currently a velocity comp on the headline payroll signal.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: Almost Seattle-level AI-exposed private employment share.

signals.label: R&D density
signals.detail: The strongest R&D share in this comparison, and the reason Boston belongs here.

regions.name: Chicago MSA
regions.verdict: Huge scale, modest velocity, finance-heavy exposed base.
regions.read: Chicago is useful because it is a giant diversified incumbent, not because it is the future model. It gives South Florida a mega-market comparison for scale, wages, logistics, finance, and institutional depth.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: The largest labor pool in the domestic comparison.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: Stable, but nowhere near Austin or Orlando velocity.

signals.label: Wage base
signals.detail: Higher covered wage level than Florida metros except South Florida.

signals.label: Data caveat
signals.detail: Several detailed tech industries are suppressed in QCEW, so Chicago should be read as scale and finance/logistics depth, not a clean software comparison.

regions.name: Nashville MSA
regions.verdict: Fast growth and tight unemployment, but not a software benchmark.
regions.read: Nashville belongs because it is a Southeast growth and healthcare-services competitor. It is not a Seattle or Austin on AI depth, but it is a relevant warning on migration, healthcare HQs, and state-level execution.

signals.label: Scale
signals.detail: Smaller than the major Florida metros, but large enough to compete for healthcare and HQ projects.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: A stronger five-year payroll signal than South Florida, Tampa, Jacksonville, Seattle, Boston, or Chicago.

signals.label: Labor tightness
signals.detail: The tightest March 2026 unemployment rate in this domestic set.

signals.label: AI caveat
signals.detail: Nashville's lesson is applied AI in healthcare services and HQ operations, not raw software density.

internationalMetroComparison.headline: Miami's international comp set is not New York. It is Dubai, Riyadh, Taipei, and Singapore.
internationalMetroComparison.summary: These are not apples-to-apples labor-market comparisons. They are operating-model comparisons: gateway capital, sovereign execution, semiconductor supply chain, and regulated global hub discipline. Miami's job is to borrow the right mechanism from each without losing its Florida advantage.

regions.name: Miami / South Florida
regions.verdict: The global gateway is real. The execution stack has to get sharper.
regions.read: Miami has wealth migration, LATAM connectivity, finance, trade, real estate, hospitality, and cultural magnetism. The next step is turning that into an institutional operating system for applied AI, capital formation, and hemispheric headquarters.

signals.label: Gateway
signals.detail: Miami's native global lane is hemispheric: finance, trade, founders, real estate, healthcare, and family office capital.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: The market has energy and inflow, but the economic-development machine is less coordinated than Dubai, Riyadh, or Singapore.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: The best Miami lane is applied AI for finance, insurance, LATAM trade, logistics, healthcare, hospitality, and public services.

signals.label: Risk
signals.detail: Without a sharper project ledger, talent strategy, and infrastructure posture, Miami stays a lifestyle gateway rather than a productivity hub.

regions.name: Dubai
regions.verdict: The benchmark for brand, speed, capital attraction, and coordinated execution.
regions.read: Dubai is the cleanest international comparison for Miami because it competes as a gateway, not as a traditional industrial city. The lesson is not copy-paste incentives. The lesson is unified ambition, service speed, global investor UX, and an unmistakable city strategy.

signals.label: Ambition
signals.detail: Dubai's D33 agenda explicitly targets doubling the economy and ranking among top global cities.

signals.label: Gateway
signals.detail: Dubai runs the global-gateway play with a much clearer operating system than Miami currently has.

signals.label: Execution
signals.detail: The governance and investor-service stack is easier to read than fragmented U.S. metro governance.

signals.label: Miami lesson
signals.detail: Miami should translate the Dubai playbook into a LATAM and Florida Brain operating system, not mimic Gulf real estate economics.

regions.name: Riyadh
regions.verdict: The benchmark for state-backed ambition and project scale.
regions.read: Riyadh is useful because it shows what happens when national strategy, capital, infrastructure, and place-making are pointed in one direction. Miami cannot and should not copy that model, but Florida can learn from the seriousness of project delivery.

signals.label: Capital stack
signals.detail: Saudi Vision 2030 puts diversification, investment, infrastructure, and global positioning into one national frame.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: Riyadh competes through large, visible, state-backed projects and institutional urgency.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: The AI angle is less organic software density and more capital, infrastructure, energy, and sovereign execution.

signals.label: Miami lesson
signals.detail: Florida needs named projects, conversion tracking, and delivery accountability if it wants to be taken seriously at this scale.

regions.name: Taipei / Taiwan tech corridor
regions.verdict: The benchmark for semiconductor and AI-hardware centrality.
regions.read: Taipei is not a Miami lifestyle comp. It is the AI supply-chain comp. Taiwan's lesson is how hardware, suppliers, advanced packaging, testing, engineering talent, and government strategy turn AI demand into national economic leverage.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: Taiwan sits near the center of AI hardware, HPC, server, semiconductor, and advanced packaging supply chains.

signals.label: Velocity
signals.detail: Global AI infrastructure demand is pulling investment through Taiwan's supplier ecosystem.

signals.label: Risk
signals.detail: Taiwan's advantage comes with grid, security, and geopolitical constraints that Florida should study carefully.

signals.label: Miami lesson
signals.detail: Florida does not need to become Taiwan, but it does need to choose where it fits in AI hardware, power, cooling, space, defense, and logistics.

regions.name: Singapore
regions.verdict: The benchmark for trusted hub discipline.
regions.read: Singapore is the best comparison for disciplined, source-aware, globally trusted economic development. It combines capital, talent, regulation, logistics, advanced manufacturing, and digital infrastructure into a managed hub strategy.

signals.label: Hub discipline
signals.detail: Singapore's advantage is reliability: law, talent, logistics, finance, data, and government coordination.

signals.label: AI base
signals.detail: Singapore is actively positioning around AI-related semiconductor demand, advanced packaging, R&D, and sustainable data-center growth.

signals.label: Execution
signals.detail: The public-sector and investment-promotion machine is tighter than most U.S. metro structures.

signals.label: Miami lesson
signals.detail: For Miami, global finance and LATAM gateway ambitions need transparent data, source discipline, speed, and institutional reliability.

fdiScoreboard.headline: Florida is top-tier by FDI reach, but not yet by capital intensity.
fdiScoreboard.summary: The archive memo says Florida ranks with the national leaders on FDI jobs and greenfield project count, while BEA's 2024 state tables show the sharper delta: Florida led current employment from new FDI, but Texas and Georgia are gaining the capex flow.

observatory.headline: Four-score FDI Observatory
observatory.summary: Stock shows accumulated foreign-owned footprint. Flow shows the newest annual capital. Quality separates greenfield and strategic work from acquisition churn. Pipeline tests whether announcements convert into real jobs, capex, and operating sites.

scores.label: Stock
scores.delta: Florida is $341.8B below Texas in the archive foreign-owned PP&E baseline.
scores.read: Florida has real foreign-company scale, but the installed capital base is not yet a Texas-style heavy-industry or infrastructure base.

scores.label: Flow
scores.delta: Florida first-year new FDI expenditures fell 7.8% YoY while Texas rose 80.5% and Georgia rose 154.8%.
scores.read: Florida is still winning deals, but the newest annual capital flow says competitors are converting the capex boom faster.

scores.label: Quality
scores.delta: Florida current employment from new FDI jumped to 32.7k, but 2024 greenfield current employment rounded to zero.
scores.read: The jobs headline is excellent, but quality scoring has to ask whether those are new production footprints or acquired existing payrolls.

scores.label: Pipeline
scores.delta: Strong announced project count, but the next step is conversion scoring against jobs, capex, wage, and operating status.
scores.read: The WAVTEQ, FPL, SelectFlorida, Outlook, and Dropbox archive can become the forward-looking pipeline if every announcement gets a confidence score and follow-through status.

deltas.read: Texas is the 2024 capex leader. The growth is acquisition-heavy, but the scale is what Florida has to compete against.
Georgia is the clean warning light: lower long-run FDI scale than Florida, but 2024 flow surged because the project machine is sharper.
California still has the deepest global-company base, but 2024 new FDI flow and current employment both moved down.
North Carolina's expenditure flow accelerated, but BEA suppressions keep the employment and greenfield-quality read incomplete.
Florida led current employment from new FDI in 2024, but first-year expenditures slipped and the employment jump appears acquisition-heavy.
New York's annual capital flow fell hard, but current employment still rose, consistent with a services and acquisition-heavy profile.
Tennessee's 2024 first-year flow was tiny after a weak 2023, even though its long-run manufacturing FDI posture remains strategically relevant.

metrics.label: FDI jobs
metrics.read: Florida has real FDI scale and leads regional peers on jobs. The question is whether those jobs sit in sectors that lift the wage curve.

metrics.label: Greenfield projects
metrics.read: Project count is strong. The weakness is project weight, industrial depth, and mega-project capital intensity.

metrics.label: FDI stock
metrics.read: The capital stock gap versus Texas is the cleanest proof that Florida's FDI mix skews less heavy-industrial.

metrics.label: First-year expenditures
metrics.read: Momentum exists, but the memo says most recent inflow came mainly through acquisitions rather than new establishments.

states.name: California
states.capitalIntensityRead: Largest jobs and project scale in the comparison set, with deep tech and R&D density.
states.posture: Benchmark for tech, R&D, and global-company depth.

states.name: Texas
states.capitalIntensityRead: Dominates the capital stock comparison with energy, industrial, and large-project depth.
states.posture: The peer Florida has to beat for heavy capex.

states.name: New York
states.capitalIntensityRead: Deep services, finance, and foreign-company presence.
states.posture: Useful HQ, services, and source-country benchmark.

states.name: Florida
states.capitalIntensityRead: Leads regional peers on jobs and projects, but capital stock is far below Texas.
states.posture: Strong FDI reach, weaker mega-project intensity.

states.name: North Carolina
states.capitalIntensityRead: Fewer projects than Florida but stronger fit with advanced manufacturing and mega-project recruitment.
states.posture: Watch for advanced manufacturing and training alignment.

states.name: Georgia
states.capitalIntensityRead: Lower FDI jobs than Florida but sharper manufacturing, logistics, EV, and supplier posture.
states.posture: Watch EV, battery, port, and supplier wins.

states.name: Tennessee
states.capitalIntensityRead: Smaller scale, but auto and Japanese FDI make it strategically important.
states.posture: A reminder that project quality can outweigh project count.

policyToolkit.headline: Competitor states are not just cheaper. They are tooled.
policyToolkit.summary: The 2020 competitor-state report shows why Florida's next growth argument cannot stop at tax climate. The winning states package site readiness, training, infrastructure, local deal authority, data-center exemptions, and mega-project tools into one project machine.

states.competitorSignal: Florida has useful tools, but they are not yet framed as a next-economy operating system.
tools: Semiconductor, defense, and space technology exemption
states.floridaGap: The toolkit exists. The open question is whether it is fast and specific enough for AI compute, semiconductors, advanced manufacturing, and large capex projects.

states.competitorSignal: Texas pairs state deal-closing authority with local agreements and a clearer data-center exemption frame.
tools: Data center state sales and use tax exemption
states.floridaGap: Texas makes it easier to see the capital-stack answer for power-heavy projects.

states.competitorSignal: North Carolina's ledger combines data-center exemptions, JDIG, One NC, infrastructure, reuse, and rail access.
tools: Data center sales and use tax exemptions
states.floridaGap: North Carolina is a useful model for aligning recruitment tools with manufacturing, rural infrastructure, and workforce delivery.

states.competitorSignal: Georgia's toolkit is built around job credits, quality jobs, mega-projects, investment, and R&D.
states.floridaGap: Georgia's lesson is not just incentives. It is a clearer manufacturing and supplier-chain posture.

states.competitorSignal: Tennessee uses FastTrack and job credits to make manufacturing projects feel executable.
states.floridaGap: Tennessee shows how a smaller state can punch above its weight when the training and industrial machinery answer is legible.

states.competitorSignal: Western peers frame data centers, qualified facilities, R&D, and infrastructure as a scale-up package.
tools: Utah High Cost Infrastructure Tax Credit
Utah life science and technology credit
states.floridaGap: The AI capex race rewards states that can answer power, water, infrastructure, and post-performance incentives in one conversation.

institutionalCapacity.headline: The old EFI BI memo is the blueprint for Florida Brain.
institutionalCapacity.summary: The archived side-by-side says EFI had useful baseline data, but it was trapped in static PDFs, scattered research roles, and underused data systems. Floridanomics is the product answer to that institutional gap.

metrics.label: Existing research bench
metrics.context: EFI research roles described in September 2022
metrics.read: The memo says research capacity existed, but it was non-managerial and spread across business development, ITD, and military-defense work.

metrics.label: BI target bench
metrics.context: Proposed VP, manager, and combined research structure
metrics.read: The proposal was not just more staff. It was a shift from static reporting to business-intelligence products.

metrics.label: Legacy CRM depth
metrics.context: Salesforce data described as complex, layered, and needing cleanup
metrics.read: The archive warns that dashboards are only as good as their data architecture. Florida Brain needs source discipline from day one.

metrics.label: Data stack named
metrics.context: WISERTrade, BEA, JobsEQ, EMSI, Kompass, CoStar, D&B Hoovers, IBISWorld, and more
metrics.read: The state already knew the right direction: live tools, better visualization, FDI market intelligence, and dynamic web dashboards.

operatingLessons: Do not publish static PDF dashboards when the underlying question is moving monthly.
Treat Salesforce, trade, FDI, incentives, and project pipelines as one intelligence fabric.
Separate public claims from internal leads so Florida Brain can be bold without losing source integrity.
Build a competitor-state update rhythm before the next big capex cycle becomes old news.

migration.headline: Migration is Florida's flywheel. It is not the whole strategy.
migration.summary: The internal migration spreadsheet shows Florida beating Texas on state-to-state migration in 2021 and 2022. That is the demand engine. The competitor tool asks whether the supply side is becoming high-wage enough.
migration.read: Florida can win people and still lose the next-economy mix. The model has to track whether migration becomes taxable income, technical workforce, founder formation, and strategic investment.

semiconductor.headline: Florida has a chips ledger. Now it needs a compute-and-supply-chain thesis.
semiconductor.summary: The 2024 spreadsheet logs $391,985,340 of Florida semiconductor-related commitments across DOE, JGGF, UCF, BRIDG, and UF line items. The next layer should connect those commitments to the AI capex, aerospace, and advanced-manufacturing map.

commitments.label: DOE high schools, tech colleges, and state colleges
JGGF 2023 NeoCity
commitments.context: Job Growth Grant Fund semiconductor ecosystem line item

commitments.label: JGGF 2023 Valencia College
commitments.context: Workforce and technical training line item

commitments.label: JGGF 2023 Santa Fe College
commitments.context: Workforce and technical training line item

commitments.label: JGGF 2023 Lake Technical College
commitments.context: Workforce and technical training line item

semiconductor.read: The commitment stack is real enough to build around. The missing product is a live comparison against peer-state chips, data-center, aerospace, and advanced-manufacturing commitments.

nextMoves: Refresh the FDI scoreboard against current BEA, SelectUSA, and fDi Markets sources before public citation.
Convert the 2020 incentive report into a structured state-policy ledger with current URLs, eligibility thresholds, and sunset dates.
Build a named project ledger for Texas, Georgia, North Carolina, Arizona, Utah, and Tennessee AI capex, chips, EV, battery, and aerospace wins.
Pair migration with IRS income migration, BLS wage mix, housing constraints, and tax-base yield so the flywheel is measured, not assumed.
Add a Florida Model export mode that turns this competitor layer into sponsor-ready HTML briefs for Florida Brain.

federal.headline: Federal data spine
federal.summary: BLS, Census exports, and BEA are live when their keys are present. Census business applications, EIA, and IRS remain explicit feed contracts so the dashboard can separate live federal metrics from safe fallbacks and download-only official sources.

sources.label: BLS Public Data API
sources.note: Live LAUS and CES labor-market backbone. Key is optional but raises BLS request limits.

sources.label: Business Formation Statistics API
sources.note: Florida business applications remain on the Census BFS series via FRED while the state-level Census BFS API path is confirmed.

sources.label: State export API
sources.note: Direct source for state exports by month. Until the key is configured, the dashboard keeps the verified SelectFlorida trade release.

sources.label: BEA Regional API
sources.note: Direct source for state GDP and income accounts. Falls back to the BEA/FRED bridge when the key is unavailable.

sources.label: EIA Open Data API
sources.note: Direct source for electricity price, sales, and power-readiness proxies tied to data-center competitiveness.

sources.label: IRS SOI Migration Data
sources.note: Official migration and AGI flow data. IRS publishes downloadable files rather than a stable JSON API.

signals.label: Florida unemployment
signals.read: The labor-market cockpit is already on live BLS LAUS data.

signals.label: Florida nonfarm payrolls
signals.read: Payrolls are live from BLS CES, which is the right monthly benchmark for jobs by employer.

signals.label: Peer payroll leader
signals.read: The competitor tab already compares Florida against peers on the same federal labor-series footing.

signals.label: Business applications
signals.read: Business formation is federal Census BFS data, but Florida state values remain on the FRED bridge until a state-level Census BFS API path is confirmed.
signals.caveat: Census key is installed; BFS remains a FRED bridge because the confirmed Census API path is national, not Florida state-level.

signals.label: Florida exports
signals.read: The trade layer can now reconcile the SelectFlorida release against the Census state export API.

signals.label: Real gross state product
signals.read: Real GSP is now coming straight from the BEA Regional API, table SAGDP9, line 1, Florida.

signals.label: Industrial electricity price
signals.read: Power-cost monitoring is live, which matters for AI infrastructure, advanced manufacturing, and data-center competitiveness.

signals.label: Income migration
signals.read: IRS SOI is the right official layer for income migration, but it is a download pipeline rather than a clean JSON endpoint.
signals.caveat: Build a separate IRS SOI ingest job for county-to-county and state-to-state migration files.

nextFeeds: Extend Census exports from statewide totals into monthly commodity and partner-market detail.
Confirm a Census state-level BFS API path or replace the FRED bridge with an official Census CSV ingest.
Extend BEA Regional API coverage into personal income, GDP by industry, and metro/county output layers.
Extend EIA feeds from industrial price into sales, load, generation mix, and interconnection proxy data.
Build the IRS SOI download ingest for income migration and AGI flows by state and county.

terminal.headline: Export the Florida model as an operating system, not a slogan.
terminal.thesis: Florida's advantage is not one metric. It is migration, business formation, low-tax discipline, gateway infrastructure, aerospace cadence, and policy restraint working together. The open question is whether that model can also win the power-heavy AI capex cycle without making households subsidize it.
terminal.operatingQuestion: Is Florida converting population and capital inflow into high-wage strategic industries faster than peer states, or is it letting the largest capex boom in modern infrastructure compound somewhere else?

sources.label: BLS April 2026 state release
sources.note: Florida posted the largest monthly payroll gain in April 2026, while its unemployment rate rose 1.1 percentage points year over year.

sources.label: CBRE H2 2025 data center trends
sources.note: Tracks primary-market absorption, Dallas momentum, power constraints, inference demand, and the national incentive race.

sources.label: JLL 2026 global data center outlook
sources.note: Frames the global AI infrastructure supercycle as capacity moving from 103 GW to 200 GW by 2030 with up to $3T of investment.

sources.label: Florida Governor SB 484 release
sources.note: Official ratepayer-protection and local-authority frame for Florida data-center policy.

sources.label: Florida Senate SB 484 summary
sources.note: Large-load customers must pay cost of service and cannot shift nonpayment risk to the general body of ratepayers.

sources.label: Space Florida aerospace readout
sources.note: Source for launch cadence and aerospace project-pipeline context.

sources.label: Blue Origin Florida expansion
sources.note: Project-level source for the $600M Cape Canaveral manufacturing expansion and 500 high-wage jobs frame.

sources.label: PortMiami cargo statistics
sources.note: Official gateway metrics for tonnage, TEUs, and Americas trade capacity.

sources.label: Port Everglades cargo statistics
sources.note: Official cargo and refrigerated-container metrics for the South Florida logistics layer.

sources.label: Florida Council of 100 Ambition Accelerated
sources.note: Business-led ambition frame for Florida's next-economy strategy.

sources.label: Florida Chamber income migration analysis
sources.note: Canonical public source for the $4M-plus per hour net income-migration frame.

sources.label: Florida TaxWatch
sources.note: Florida fiscal and competitiveness research source for future policy memos.

sources.label: James Madison Institute
sources.note: Florida economic-freedom and regulatory-policy source for future policy memos.

aiCapexIndex.label: AI Capex Gap Index
aiCapexIndex.caveat: Prototype editorial index. It is source-backed but not an official state score until Florida-specific MW pipeline, interconnection, power-price, and project-award data are wired.

metrics.label: Florida payroll pulse
metrics.context: April 2026 monthly nonfarm payroll change, largest among states
metrics.read: The labor market is not collapsing. The better question is composition: are the new jobs part of the next capex cycle or mostly population-serving growth?

metrics.label: Unemployment warning light
metrics.context: Florida April 2026 unemployment rate, up 1.1 percentage points year over year
metrics.read: Florida is no longer telling a simple tight-labor-market story. Elevated unemployment beside a payroll jump demands a sector and wage-quality read.

metrics.label: Texas comparison
metrics.read: Texas is not proof by itself, but it is the right peer to test whether power-heavy capex is converting into labor-market resilience.

metrics.label: Dallas data-center absorption
metrics.read: This is the concrete 'eating our lunch' benchmark: hyperscale demand is landing in measurable megawatts in Texas markets.

metrics.label: AI infrastructure supercycle
metrics.context: JLL estimate for total data-center investment over the next five years
metrics.read: If the global capacity base nearly doubles by 2030, missing even a small slice is a real strategic opportunity cost.

factors.label: Demand adjacency
factors.read: Florida has population, LATAM, aerospace, finance, and edge-inference use cases that make strategic compute plausible.

factors.label: Power readiness
factors.read: The missing dataset is Florida-specific MW under construction, interconnection queue, power procurement, and self-supply capacity.

factors.label: Project visibility
factors.read: Florida can name aerospace wins clearly. It cannot yet show a comparable AI compute project ledger.

factors.label: Incentive discipline
factors.read: SB 484 is directionally sound: no household subsidy for dumb load. The upgrade is a fast lane for strategic compute that pays its own way.

factors.label: High-wage conversion
factors.read: Business formation and migration are strong, but the terminal needs OEWS, QCEW, and cluster wage data to prove the wage curve is bending.

highWageMonitor.headline: The next scorecard is not job count. It is wage-quality conversion.
highWageMonitor.summary: Florida can add jobs and still lose the next cycle if growth concentrates in lower-wage population services while AI, aerospace, chips, advanced logistics, and life-science capex land elsewhere.

metrics.label: Information jobs
metrics.read: If AI infrastructure is becoming a real Florida industry, information employment should stop behaving like a thin support sector.

metrics.label: Construction jobs
metrics.read: Data centers, aerospace factories, ports, and grid upgrades should first show up in construction and specialty-trade labor demand.

metrics.label: Professional services
metrics.read: The Florida model needs headquarters, engineering, finance, legal, and operations work attached to the physical build-out.

projectLedger.name: Blue Origin Cape Canaveral expansion
projectLedger.strategicRead: This is the model project: physical innovation, manufacturing depth, and Space Coast specialization in one investable proof point.

projectLedger.name: Space Florida aerospace pipeline
projectLedger.strategicRead: A project pipeline this large deserves to sit beside labor, supplier, and wage data so Florida can prove the cluster is deepening.

projectLedger.name: Florida strategic compute ledger
projectLedger.strategicRead: This is the glaring blank space. If peers can publish megawatts, capex, tenants, and construction timelines, Florida Brain should track the absence as aggressively as the wins.

projectLedger.name: PortMiami gateway capacity
projectLedger.strategicRead: The Americas gateway is not marketing copy. It is a physical logistics base that can support trade, finance, perishables, and regional compute demand.

projectLedger.name: Port Everglades cold-chain capacity
projectLedger.strategicRead: Cold-chain depth is part of the Florida model because gateway trade is a high-infrastructure business, not just a tourism-adjacent story.

forecasts.claim: Florida's unemployment softness may partly reflect under-capture of the AI infrastructure capex cycle, not just a normal labor-market cooldown.
forecasts.mechanism: AI infrastructure first creates power, construction, engineering, and operations demand. If those projects land in Texas, Arizona, Georgia, North Carolina, or Virginia instead of Florida, peer labor markets can look stronger even when Florida keeps winning migration.
leadingIndicators: Announced megawatts and MW under construction by state
Large-load interconnection queue and utility tariff filings
Hyperscaler, neocloud, and colocation capex announcements
laggingIndicators: Average weekly wages in target clusters
forecasts.baseCase: Florida remains a migration, formation, aerospace, and gateway state, but AI infrastructure stays under-measured and secondary.
forecasts.ambitionCase: Florida creates a strategic-compute lane tied to self-funded power, grid resilience, Space Coast, LATAM, health, and financial-services workloads.
forecasts.riskCase: Florida blocks or slows too much strategic compute while peers compound power, fiber, and high-wage operations ecosystems.
forecasts.counterCase: The unemployment increase is mainly labor-supply normalization and sector churn; Florida's monthly payroll gain shows demand is still broad enough.
forecasts.updateTrigger: Upgrade or downgrade the thesis when Florida-specific MW pipeline, utility tariff filings, or named AI infrastructure projects become source-visible.

forecasts.claim: The Florida model can travel if it is framed as a growth operating system: fiscal discipline, fast formation, migration pull, gateway assets, and strategic infrastructure.
forecasts.mechanism: Other states and countries can copy pieces of Florida, but the exportable product is the sequencing: attract people and capital, protect taxpayers, then convert that demand into clusters with measurable wage outcomes.
forecasts.baseCase: Florida Brain becomes a strong state dashboard and content engine for internal strategy.
forecasts.ambitionCase: Floridanomics becomes a reusable model for governors, chambers, economic developers, founders, and investors outside Florida.
forecasts.riskCase: The brand becomes boosterism if the dashboard cannot show the difference between population growth and productivity growth.
forecasts.counterCase: Florida's model may be too dependent on unique tax, weather, migration, and regional-gateway advantages to export cleanly.
forecasts.updateTrigger: Advance the export thesis only when the terminal can show source-linked playbooks, not just state pride.

policyMemos.title: Strategic compute, not dumb load.
policyMemos.stance: Florida should not subsidize hyperscale loads through household utility bills. It also should not confuse disciplined cost allocation with surrendering the AI infrastructure race.
policyMemos.whatChanged: SB 484 makes the ratepayer line explicit: large-load customers pay their own cost of service, local governments keep authority, and utility risk should not be shifted to the public.
policyMemos.mechanism: The policy problem is separating strategic compute that strengthens the grid, pays full freight, and creates high-wage work from passive load that raises costs without a local productivity payoff.
policyMemos.recommendation: Create a Florida strategic-compute fast lane for projects that bring their own power or grid assets, cover full marginal cost, use reclaimed water where appropriate, disclose deal terms after exemption periods, and tie incentives to high-wage operations and Florida-specific workloads.
policyMemos.whatNotToDo: Do not write blank checks, do not socialize utility costs, do not block every project, and do not copy Texas if the better Florida lane is edge, LATAM, Space Coast, health, finance, and resilient inference.
nextMoves: Add Florida-specific MW pipeline and utility tariff tracker
Create a named project ledger for data centers, grid upgrades, and advanced manufacturing
Map strategic workloads to Space Coast, LATAM, health, finance, and emergency-resilience use cases

policyMemos.title: Make wage quality the referee.
policyMemos.stance: The next Floridanomics product should judge policy by whether it bends the wage curve in target clusters, not whether it produces a bigger ribbon-cutting number.
policyMemos.whatChanged: Florida can plausibly be both a strong jobs state and a state with warning lights. That contradiction is the product opportunity.
policyMemos.mechanism: Population growth creates demand. Strategic capex creates productivity. The dashboard has to separate those two engines.
policyMemos.recommendation: Wire OEWS, QCEW, workforce credentials, target occupations, and county-level wage outcomes into the terminal before declaring a cluster strategy successful.
policyMemos.whatNotToDo: Do not let total jobs, startup counts, or migration dollars substitute for high-wage conversion.
nextMoves: Add high-wage occupation basket by cluster
Track target-cluster wages versus state median
Connect education programs and credentials to employment outcomes

evidenceBlocks.title: The exportable Florida model
evidenceBlocks.briefCopy: Florida's model is a growth flywheel: people move in, capital follows, formation accelerates, the tax base expands, and policy has to convert that demand into high-wage clusters without losing fiscal discipline.
evidenceBlocks.exportUse: Use this as the opening frame for a global Florida Model brief, investor deck, or governor-level policy memo.

evidenceBlocks.title: The uncomfortable AI question
evidenceBlocks.briefCopy: Florida is winning people and payrolls, but the AI infrastructure boom is measured in megawatts, power procurement, construction pipelines, and high-wage operations. If that ledger is blank, it is not neutral.
evidenceBlocks.exportUse: Use this as the thesis paragraph for the AI Capex Gap briefing page and the next Florida Brain issue.

evidenceBlocks.title: The policy discipline frame
evidenceBlocks.briefCopy: The pro-growth answer is not subsidy or refusal. It is a disciplined market design: strategic compute pays full cost, strengthens infrastructure, and earns speed only when the public does not carry the bill.
evidenceBlocks.exportUse: Use this as the sound policy spine for op-eds, testimony, and chamber-facing memos.

evidenceBlocks.title: Physical innovation is Florida's lane
evidenceBlocks.briefCopy: Florida's most distinctive innovation assets are physical: ports, launches, aerospace manufacturing, logistics, health, and energy-resilient edge demand. The dashboard should make that visible.
evidenceBlocks.exportUse: Use this to move Florida Brain beyond generic startup ecosystem coverage.

scorecard2030.title: The Florida Chamber scoreboard says the state is bigger, richer, and still short of its 2030 finish line.
scorecard2030.summary: This section brings the Florida Scorecard into the app as a real source. It ties the size of the current economy to the state's explicit 2030 targets instead of leaving the growth thesis as generic branding.

stats.label: Gross domestic product
stats.context: Florida Scorecard statewide GDP, improving

stats.label: Global economy rank
stats.context: If Florida were a country, based on a 2024 GDP baseline of $1.73T
stats.note: Inference: Florida's 2024 GDP baseline of $1.73T sits between Australia and Spain in the World Bank's 2024 current-dollar country table.

stats.label: 2030 jobs gap
stats.context: Net new jobs needed by 2030, State of Florida FutureCast

stats.label: Business startups
stats.context: March 2026, ranked #1 nationally

stats.label: Income migration
stats.context: Florida Chamber Foundation annual net income migration frame
stats.note: The Chamber's $36.05B annual figure annualizes to more than $4M per hour. The Scorecard currently shows a lower IRS-based hourly figure without a public note explaining the gap.

interpretation: The scorecard matters because it turns the Florida thesis into a scoreboard with explicit finish lines. Florida is already enormous, but the gap between rank 4 state GDP today and a number-one ambition by 2030 is still real.

The cleanest growth signal in this stack is not a vibe metric. It is business formation staying first nationally while the Chamber's public migration frame still clears four million dollars per hour into the state.

sources.label: Florida Scorecard, statewide innovation and economic development metrics
Florida Scorecard, State of Florida FutureCast
Florida Chamber Foundation income migration analysis
World Bank GDP current US dollars API

snowbirdIndex.title: Seasonal wealth migration is not a tourism story. This is a proxy until the Q4/Q1 snowbird series is wired.
snowbirdIndex.summary: This proxy build uses Florida Chamber migration metrics and retirement-county pull to show why the state keeps carrying housing, health care, and infrastructure pressure even when payroll growth softens.

stats.label: State income migration
stats.context: Florida Chamber Foundation annual net income migration frame
stats.note: The Chamber's $36.05B annual figure annualizes to more than $4M per hour. The Scorecard currently shows a lower IRS-based hourly figure without a public note explaining the gap.

stats.label: Collier pull
stats.context: Collier County income migration, Florida Scorecard

stats.label: Sumter pull
stats.context: Sumter County income migration, Florida Scorecard

stats.label: 2030 population range
stats.context: Statewide 2030 range, State of Florida FutureCast

interpretation: Florida's seasonal population story shows up as capital first. When Collier and Sumter pull that much income, the downstream pressure hits insurance, health care, housing supply, and local roads long before it shows up in a beach photo.
This is not a Miami-only story. The retirement counties help explain why statewide demand stays hot even when payroll momentum cools.

sources.label: Florida Chamber Foundation income migration analysis
Florida Scorecard, State of Florida FutureCast

spaceCoastCadence.title: Florida's innovation story is physical before it is financial.
spaceCoastCadence.summary: Launches, spaceport investment, and aerospace build-out are all rising in the same corridor. This is the part of the Florida innovation thesis that generic startup dashboards usually miss.

stats.label: Launches in 2025
stats.context: Record-breaking 2025 launch count, Space Florida

stats.label: Aerospace pipeline
stats.context: 220-project pipeline, Space Florida 2025 readout

stats.label: Economic impact
stats.context: Space Florida impact since 2007

stats.label: Blue Origin expansion
stats.context: Cape Canaveral manufacturing project announced May 22, 2026

interpretation: The Space Coast matters because it turns aerospace from a branding asset into an operating system for manufacturing, dual-use, and infrastructure spending.
When launch cadence, project pipeline, and factory investment all rise together, Florida stops looking like a tourism economy with rockets on the side.

sources.label: Space Florida 2025 aerospace pipeline and launch update
Florida governor announcement on Blue Origin's Cape Canaveral expansion
Reuters on Blue Origin's Cape Canaveral expansion

latamGateway.title: Florida's gateway claim is physical, not rhetorical.
latamGateway.summary: The South Florida trade corridor is doing real freight work for Latin America and the Caribbean. This section brings PortMiami and Port Everglades into the trade story instead of treating the gateway line as a slogan.

stats.label: PortMiami tonnage
stats.context: FY2024 total tonnage, official PortMiami snapshot

stats.label: PortMiami container flow
stats.context: FY2024 TEUs, official PortMiami snapshot

stats.label: LATAM + Caribbean share
stats.context: PortMiami regional trade share, FY2025

stats.label: Port Everglades refrigerated cargo
stats.context: FY2024 refrigerated cargo leader in Florida

stats.label: Port Everglades container flow
stats.context: FY2024 official cargo statistics

interpretation: PortMiami says almost half of its trade is tied to Latin America and the Caribbean, while Port Everglades is moving more than a million TEUs and leading Florida in refrigerated cargo. The gateway thesis is sitting on the dock, not in a keynote deck.
That matters because South Florida wins on adjacency. The same corridor handles containers, perishables, finance, and commercial relationships that keep Florida plugged into the Americas.

trade.headline: Florida Exports Hit $78.9B in 2025 - Largest Year in History
trade.releaseTitle: Florida Businesses Set Record High Export Sales in 2025

heroMetrics.label: Total Florida Exports (2025)
heroMetrics.helper: Largest export year in Florida history

heroMetrics.label: Bilateral Trade via FL Ports + Airports

deltas.label: Manufactured Export Share
deltas.baseLabel: 92% of all FL-origin exports are manufactured products

narrative.headline: Florida's export engine is firing on every cylinder - and 92% of it is built here.
whatStandsOut: $78.9B is the largest export year in Florida history - up $6.4B (+8.9%) vs 2024 and $21.6B (+37.8%) vs 2018.
Manufactured products = 92% of all Florida-origin exports ($72.2B). Florida's manufacturing sector is the fastest-growing in the country.
3 of Florida's top 5 export markets are in LATAM (Brazil, Mexico, Colombia) - reinforcing Florida as the Americas' commercial gateway.
Civilian aircraft and parts alone shipped $12.8B - aerospace is the single biggest export category.
watchOuts: 92% manufacturing concentration means the headline is sensitive to single-vertical shocks (aerospace cycle, pharma, telecom).
LATAM exposure (3 of top 5 markets) carries FX, political, and tariff-posture risk worth tracking quarter to quarter.
whyItMatters: $205B in bilateral trade through Florida seaports and airports reinforces the state's logistics-gateway position.
$1.9B in SelectFlorida-generated exports in FY24-25 (+$500M YoY) is direct, measurable ROI on Florida's trade-mission infrastructure.
A single trade show - Singapore Airshow Feb 2026 - drove $150M+ in reported sales for participating Florida companies.

leading.headline: Ahead of the print.
leading.summary: Official labor data arrives about six weeks after the fact. These signals move first: postings lead hiring, claims lead the unemployment rate, and permits lead construction payrolls.

signals.label: Job postings index
signals.leads: Leads hiring; payroll prints follow postings by months.

signals.label: Initial unemployment claims
signals.leads: Leads the unemployment rate by roughly six weeks.

signals.label: Continued unemployment claims
signals.leads: Confirms whether laid-off workers are being reabsorbed.

signals.label: Building permits (units)
signals.leads: Leads construction employment and the housing supply pipeline.

benchmarks.headline: The cost of operating, against the same eight states.
benchmarks.summary: Site decisions come down to operating math. Average weekly wages show what labor costs across the peer set; industrial power prices show what the capex-heavy projects will pay to run.
